content.problemStatement:
Given a binary tree, check whether it is a structural mirror of itself (i.e., structurally symmetric around its center).

For example, the following binary tree is structurally symmetric:


Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer NNN — the number of nodes in the binary tree.
Next N−1N - 1N−1 lines contain three space separated characters pi,ci,Rip_i, c_i, R_ipi​,ci​,Ri​, describing the tree with edge informations - pip_ipi​ is an integer denoting the parent node of the iiith edge, cic_ici​ is child node, and the letter RiR_iRi​ denotes whether the child is left child or right child, it's value is `L` if cic_ici​ is left child of pip_ipi​, else `R`.


Output Format
Output on the single line, `YES` if the given binary tree is structurally symmetric to itself, `NO` otherwise.
constraints: All pi and ci are distinct across a test case
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 10) – the number of test cases. For each test case:
- The first line contains an integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 10000) – the number of nodes in the binary tree.
- The next N‑1 lines each contain three space‑separated values pi ci Ri, where pi is the parent node id, ci is the child node id, and Ri is either 'L' (ci is the left child of pi) or 'R' (ci is the right child of pi). All node ids are distinct.
The tree is guaranteed to be a valid binary tree.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if the tree is structurally symmetric (a mirror of itself) or "NO" otherwise.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The tree is perfectly symmetric: left subtree of node 1 mirrors the right subtree.
Both sub‑trees of the root have the same shape, therefore the whole tree is symmetric.
A root with one left and one right child is symmetric regardless of the values.

solution.approach: For each test case we build the tree using a hash map from node id to a node structure containing left and right pointers. While reading edges we also mark every node that appears as a child. After all edges are read, the node that never appeared as a child is the root. To check symmetry we use a recursive function isMirror(left, right) that returns true when both sub‑trees are empty, or when the left subtree of the left node mirrors the right subtree of the right node **and** the right subtree of the left node mirrors the left subtree of the right node. The whole tree is symmetric iff isMirror(root.left, root.right) is true. The algorithm runs in O(N) time per test case and uses O(N) additional memory for the nodes and the hash map.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the number of nodes in the test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) per test case for storing the nodes and the auxiliary hash map.
